rounds_summary.attempted_route: pd.read_excel MIST3, hard-coded columns ['TP9','AF7','AF8','TP10'], mean as HR proxy, pd.to_datetime(unit='s'), 30s baseline window, min-onset CFT calc
rounds_summary.observation: KeyError: None of ['TP9','AF7','AF8','TP10'] in columns — actual sheet column names did not match hard-coded strings, script crashed at column selection

rounds_summary.attempted_route: Dynamic column discovery (strip whitespace, exclude 'Right AUX'), pd.to_datetime(float_series, unit='s', utc=True), 60s baseline, bp.protocols.CFT try/except fallback
rounds_summary.observation: OutOfBoundsDatetime: timestamp value ~4.98e13 overflows nanosecond int64; pd.to_datetime with unit='s' on that float exceeds pandas M8[ns] bounds

rounds_summary.attempted_route: Same dynamic column discovery, cast timestamp to np.int64 before pd.to_datetime(unit='s', utc=True) to remove sub-second float precision; dropped biopsykit dependency
rounds_summary.observation: Same OutOfBoundsDatetime crash at identical traceback line — int64 cast does not fix overflow because the raw value ~4.98e13 is still out of range for seconds-unit nanosecond datetime

ruled_out_routes: Hard-coded column names ['TP9','AF7','AF8','TP10'] with pd.read_excel — actual sheet headers differ
pd.to_datetime(float_series, unit='s', utc=True) on raw timestamp column — overflows nanosecond dtype
pd.to_datetime(ts.astype(np.int64), unit='s', utc=True) — int64 cast does not prevent OutOfBoundsDatetime when raw values are ~4.98e13
bp.protocols.CFT() instantiation as calculation driver — never reached due to timestamp crash in rounds 2-3
synthesised_diagnosis: All three rounds failed because the 'timestamps' column in MIST3 is NOT Unix seconds: its raw values (~4.98e13) are approximately 50 billion seconds, which overflows pandas nanosecond datetime. The values are consistent with Unix milliseconds (~year 2000). Round 1 never reached timestamp parsing (column name mismatch); rounds 2-3 fixed that but both crashed on the same overflow because neither tried unit='ms' or a non-datetime numeric index.
rationale: The timestamp magnitude ~4.98e10 (after /1000) fits Unix seconds for year 2000-era data, confirming millisecond encoding. Every crash traces to assuming unit='s'; the CFT logic itself (mean baseline, min onset, percent change) was structurally sound in all rounds.
recommended_alternative: After pd.read_excel with dynamic column discovery and whitespace stripping, parse timestamps as milliseconds: use pd.to_datetime(pd.to_numeric(df[timestamp_col], errors='coerce'), unit='ms', utc=True). If that still overflows, fall back to a plain numeric offset index: subtract the first timestamp value and work in seconds arithmetically (no DatetimeIndex needed). Then apply the existing 60s baseline/min-onset CFT formula unchanged.
